title: List Operations
description: Add, remove, and modify list elements.

slides.title: Modifying Lists 🔧
slides.content:
# List Methods

Lists have powerful methods:

* `append()` — Add to end
* `insert()` — Add at position
* `remove()` — Remove by value
* `pop()` — Remove by index
* `clear()` — Remove all

slides.title: Adding Items 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
fruits = ["apple", "banana"]
print("Start:", fruits)

fruits.append("cherry")
print("After append:", fruits)

fruits.insert(1, "blueberry")
print("After insert at 1:", fruits)

fruits.extend(["date", "elderberry"])
print("After extend:", fruits)

quizData.question: Where does append() add items?
quizData.explanation: append() always adds to the END of the list!

slides.title: Removing Items 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 3]
print("Start:", nums)

nums.remove(3)  # Removes first 3
print("After remove(3):", nums)

popped = nums.pop()  # Remove last
print("After pop():", nums, "- Popped:", popped)

popped = nums.pop(1)  # Remove at index
print("After pop(1):", nums, "- Popped:", popped)

quizData.question: What's the difference between remove() and pop()?

options.text: remove uses value, pop uses index
pop uses value, remove uses index

quizData.explanation: remove(value) removes by value, pop(index) removes by position!

slides.title: List Operators ➕
slides.content:
# Combining Lists

```python
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [4, 5, 6]

print(a + b)     # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
print(a * 2)     # [1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3]
print(3 in a)    # True
```

slides.title: List Operations 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
list1 = [1, 2, 3]
list2 = [4, 5, 6]

# Concatenation
combined = list1 + list2
print("Combined:", combined)

# Repetition
repeated = [0] * 5
print("Repeated:", repeated)

# Membership
print("2 in list1:", 2 in list1)
print("7 in list1:", 7 in list1)

slides.title: Sorting Lists 📊
slides.content:
# Organizing Data

```python
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]

nums.sort()           # In-place
print(nums)           # [1, 1, 3, 4, 5, 9]

nums.sort(reverse=True)  # Descending
print(nums)           # [9, 5, 4, 3, 1, 1]
```

slides.title: Sorting Examples 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
numbers = [5, 2, 8, 1, 9]
print("Original:", numbers)

numbers.sort()
print("Sorted:", numbers)

numbers.sort(reverse=True)
print("Descending:", numbers)

words = ["banana", "Apple", "cherry"]
words.sort(key=str.lower)  # Case-insensitive
print("Words:", words)

quizData.question: Does sort() return a new list?
quizData.explanation: sort() modifies the list in place and returns None. Use sorted() for a new list!

slides.title: Other Useful Methods 📋
slides.content:
# More List Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `count(x)` | Count occurrences of x |
| `index(x)` | Find position of x |
| `reverse()` | Reverse in place |
| `copy()` | Create a copy |

slides.title: More Methods 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 2, 5]

print("Count of 2:", nums.count(2))
print("Index of 4:", nums.index(4))

nums.reverse()
print("Reversed:", nums)

copy = nums.copy()
print("Copy:", copy)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## sort() vs sorted()

```python
nums = [3, 1, 2]

# sort() modifies original, returns None
nums.sort()

# sorted() returns new list, original unchanged
new_list = sorted(nums)
```

quizData.question: What does extend() do?

options.text: Adds all items from another list

quizData.explanation: extend() adds all elements from another iterable to the list!
